d: The primary Hebrew word for father, appearing over 1,200 times. Ab denotes not only a biological father but also a grandfather, ancestor, founder of a household, or originator. God Himself is called Ab (Isaiah 63:16).
u: In ancient Israelite culture, the father held supreme authority in the household. The concept extends to tribal patriarchs. When God is called Father, it conveys His covenantal care, discipline, and tenderness. Jesus revolutionized this by inviting believers to call God Abba.
w: The root appears in many compound names: Abraham ('father of many'), Abimelech ('my father is king'). The word carries connotations of authority, provision, protection, and instruction. The plural aboth refers to ancestors.
v: Therefore a man shall leave his father and mother.

d: The Hebrew word for man or humanity, appearing about 560 times. Adam is both the proper name of the first human and the generic term for mankind. Related to adamah (ground), reflecting God's creation from dust.
u: The connection between adam and adamah is foundational to biblical anthropology. The word emphasizes both human dignity (made in God's image) and frailty (from dust, returning to dust). Ben-adam ('son of man') becomes a prophetic title.
w: The theological arc from Adam to Christ — the 'last Adam' (1 Cor 15:45) — spans Scripture. In Genesis, adam is created in God's image, falls, and is exiled; redemption restores what adam was meant to be.
v: God created man in his own image.
The LORD formed the man of dust.
What is man that you are mindful of him?

d: The Hebrew word for ground, appearing about 225 times. Closely related to adam, emphasizing cultivated land and the material from which humanity was formed. Represents both blessing and curse.
u: The wordplay between adam and adamah is central to Genesis theology. Man was taken from the ground, tasked to serve it, cursed to struggle with it. The ground itself is cursed (Gen 3:17), cries out (Gen 4:10), and awaits redemption.
w: Unlike erets (earth as territory), adamah refers to arable soil. The relationship is covenantal — obedience brings fertility, disobedience brings barrenness. Exile from the adamah replays Adam's exile from Eden.
v: The LORD formed man from the ground.
Cursed is the ground because of you.
Your brother's blood cries from the ground.
He will bless the fruit of your ground.
I will plant them on their land.

d: The Hebrew word for brother, used over 630 times. Denotes biological brother, fellow tribesman, ally, or any close associate. Brotherhood shapes Israel's understanding of community and mutual obligation.
u: Brotherhood carries covenantal weight. Love your neighbor (Lev 19:18) is literally about one's ach. Failures of brotherhood (Cain/Abel, Joseph's brothers) are cautionary. Restoration of brotherhood is prophetic hope (Ezek 37).
w: The root may relate to 'hearth,' suggesting shared domestic life. The kinsman-redeemer concept is built on ach — your brother's welfare is your responsibility. Deuteronomy's social laws repeatedly reference obligations to ach.
v: How good when brothers dwell in unity!
If there is a poor brother among you.

d: One of the most common Hebrew words, over 2,100 times. Ish denotes an individual man distinct from adam (mankind). Can mean husband, male person, soldier, or 'someone.'
u: Adam emphasizes earthy origin; ish emphasizes individual personhood. In Gen 2:23, Adam recognizes Eve as ishshah because taken from ish — a joyful exclamation of complementarity.
w: The plural anashim is irregular. Ish appears in legal texts for individual responsibility, in narrative for particular persons. Ish el-achiv ('each man to his brother') expresses mutual relationship.
v: She shall be called Woman, taken from Man.
Blessed is the man who walks not in wickedness.
He has told you, O man, what is good.

d: The Hebrew word for mother, about 220 times. Designates biological mother, grandmother, or metaphorical matriarch. Honoring one's em is part of the Decalogue (Ex 20:12).
u: Mothers shape redemptive history through faith and action. God as mother appears in Isa 66:13. Proverbs closes with the eshet chayil, embodying em as life-giver and wisdom-bearer.
w: Metaphorical uses include 'mother city' (2 Sam 20:19). In wisdom literature, em is paired with ab as source of instruction — 'Do not forsake your mother's teaching' (Prov 1:8).
v: The king rose to meet his mother.

d: One of the most frequent nouns, over 2,500 times. Erets means the entire earth, a country, the ground, or the Promised Land. Second word of the Bible — establishing the physical world as God's creation.
u: Theologically immense. The land promised to Abraham becomes tangible covenant faithfulness. Exile is ultimate curse; return is ultimate restoration. Prophets envision renewed erets filled with knowledge of the LORD.
w: Unlike adamah (soil), erets has political/covenantal connotations. 'Land of Israel' is theological, not merely geographic. 'The meek shall inherit the erets' (Ps 37:11) is quoted by Jesus.
v: Go to the land I will show you.
The earth shall be full of knowledge of the LORD.

d: The Hebrew word for woman or wife, over 780 times. Linguistically connected to ish — Adam celebrates: 'She shall be called ishshah because taken from ish.'
u: Creation presents ishshah as ezer kenegdo — 'helper corresponding to' man — a term of strength (same word describes God, Ps 121:2). Women serve as prophets, wisdom figures, covenant participants.
w: The plural nashim is irregular. In Proverbs, Lady Wisdom and the 'strange woman' compete — making ishshah a symbol of life's fundamental choice.
v: Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lappidoth.
He who finds a wife finds good.

d: One of the most versatile words, over 2,000 times. Means physical house, household/family, dynasty ('house of David'), or temple ('house of God'). Concrete expression of belonging, identity, worship.
u: Theological depth emerges in God's promise: 'The LORD will make you a house' (2 Sam 7:11) — dynasty, not building. David wanted to build God a house; God promised to build David a house. Messianic hinge.
w: Compound names: Bethlehem ('house of bread'), Bethel ('house of God'). The beth-ab ('father's house') was basic social unit. Rebuilding the house after exile symbolizes restoration of God's presence.
v: The LORD will make you a house.
I shall dwell in the house of the LORD.
As for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.

d: Most common kinship term, over 4,900 times. Means son, grandson, descendant, or group member. Ben- creates compounds: ben-adam ('son of man'), bene Yisrael ('children of Israel').
u: Sonship is biological and covenantal. Israel is God's firstborn ben (Ex 4:22). The Davidic king is God's ben (Ps 2:7). Prophetic promise of a ben points to Messiah. Sonship conveys inheritance, identity, mission.
w: Remarkably flexible. 'Son of X years' = age. 'Son of death' = deserving death. 'Sons of prophets' = prophetic guild. You are 'son of' whatever defines you.
v: You are my Son; today I have begotten you.
A wise son makes a glad father.
Children are a heritage from the LORD.

d: A verb used exclusively with God as subject in Qal, about 55 times. Describes divine creative activity bringing something genuinely new into existence. Opening verb of creation: 'In the beginning God created.'
u: Exclusivity for divine action sets it apart from asah (make) and yatsar (form). God bara heavens/earth (1:1), sea creatures (1:21), mankind (1:27). Eschatological promise: new heavens and earth (Isa 65:17).
w: Appears at three critical junctures: matter (1:1), animal life (1:21), human life (1:27) — three decisive creative acts. Theological weight cannot be overstated — existence itself is God's gift.
v: In the beginning, God created the heavens and earth.
God created man in his own image.
I create new heavens and new earth.
Create in me a clean heart, O God.

d: The primary verb for blessing, over 330 times. Means to endue with power for success, prosperity, longevity. When God blesses, He bestows favor; when humans bless God, they praise.
u: First occurrence: Gen 1:22 — God blesses creatures. By Gen 12:2-3, blessing becomes vehicle of redemption. Psalms overflow with barak toward God. Creates theological rhythm: God blesses down, humans bless up and laterally.
w: Euphemistically can mean 'curse' (Job 1:5) — scribal convention. Piel intensive form is most common. Connection to berek (knee) suggests posture of supplication.
v: The LORD bless you and keep you.
I will bless those who bless you.
I will bless the LORD at all times.

d: The Hebrew word for blessing, about 70 times. Denotes God's favor, prosperity, transmission of divine goodness. One of the most foundational biblical concepts — God intends to bless creation and through Abraham, all families.
u: Blessing is not wish but performative declaration activating God's favor. Patriarchal blessings are irrevocable speech-acts. Aaronic blessing is liturgical berakah par excellence. God's original berakah — 'be fruitful' — is life's charter.
w: Root connected to berek (knee). Berakah flows two ways: God to humanity (favor), humanity to God (praise). Later became formulaic prayer: 'Blessed are you, O LORD...'
v: I will bless you so you will be a blessing.
All these blessings shall come upon you.
The blessing of the LORD makes rich.

d: The Hebrew word for daughter, about 590 times. Feminine counterpart of ben. Extends beyond kinship to mean female inhabitant ('daughters of Zion'), group member, or measurement unit.
u: 'Daughter of Zion' is powerful prophetic metaphor for Jerusalem — in lament and joy ('Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion!' Zech 9:9). Personifies the city as woman, evoking vulnerability, beauty, divine tenderness.
w: Construct bath- forms idioms parallel to ben-: 'daughter of X years' = age, 'daughters of song' = songbirds. Stories of Zelophehad's daughters show daughters exercising initiative, receiving inheritance.
v: What can I say, O daughter of Jerusalem?
To you it shall come, O daughter of Zion.

d: Hebrew word meaning mighty one or warrior, about 160 times. From root gabar (to be strong). Describes human heroes and God — El Gibbor ('Mighty God') is messianic title (Isa 9:6).
u: David's elite warriors were gibborim (2 Sam 23). Carries ambivalence — heroes or oppressors. Applied to God, reaches highest meaning: the LORD is gibbor who saves (Zeph 3:17).
w: Root generates geburah (strength), gabar (to prevail). Messianic El Gibbor merges divine might with tenderness of a child — gospel paradox.
v: The LORD is a mighty one who will save.

d: Hebrew word for man emphasizing strength/virility, about 65 times. From g-b-r (to be strong). Unlike ish (person) or adam (human), geber highlights masculine strength.
u: Jeremiah: 'Cursed is the geber who trusts in man' (17:5); 'Blessed is the geber who trusts in the LORD' (17:7). Related gibbor is title for God.
w: Biblical masculinity paradox: true geber strength is found in trust, humility, dependence on God, not raw power.
v: Blessed is the man who trusts in the LORD.
Blessed is the man who fills his quiver.
Let the day perish when a man was born.
I am the man who has seen affliction.

d: Hebrew word for nation, about 560 times. Goy (plural goyim) originally any nation, including Israel (Gen 12:2). Over time distinguished foreign nations, eventually standard term for Gentiles.
u: God promises Abraham: great goy (Gen 12:2). Israel is goy qadosh ('holy nation,' Ex 19:6). Prophetic vision extends: all goyim stream to Zion (Isa 2:2), Servant is light to goyim (Isa 42:6).
w: Post-biblical usage became almost exclusively non-Jews, but biblical broader. Table of Nations catalogs goyim as Noah's descendants. Hope is inclusion, not destruction: 'Blessed be Egypt my people' (Isa 19:25).
v: I will make of you a great nation.
A kingdom of priests and holy nation.
I will make the nations your heritage.

d: Foundational word, over 700 times. Means way, road, or metaphorically manner of life. One of OT's most important theological metaphors — life is a journey, critical question is which derek you walk.
u: Psalms/Proverbs organize existence into two derakhim: way of righteous and way of wicked (Ps 1:6). Prophetic call: 'prepare the derek of the LORD' (Isa 40:3) — quoted in all four Gospels.
w: From d-r-k (to tread), literally trodden path. Metaphorical extension pervasive: 'way' = entire lifestyle. Derek YHWH describes both God's character and human response of covenant obedience.
v: The LORD knows the way of the righteous.
Train a child in the way he should go.
Make me to know your ways, O LORD.

d: Fundamental verb of existence, over 3,500 times. Means to be, become, exist, happen. Closely related to divine name YHWH. God says 'I AM WHO I AM' (ehyeh asher ehyeh, Ex 3:14) using first-person form.
u: Theological significance concentrated in Ex 3:14 — God who IS, WAS, WILL BE. Also structures Gen 1: divine speech causes things to be: 'Let there be light, and there was light.'
w: Unlike Greek 'to be' (copulative), Hebrew hayah emphasizes dynamic becoming. God actively IS in relation to His people. Name YHWH interpreted as 'He who causes to be' (Creator) or 'He who is' (self-existent).
v: Let there be light, and there was light.
The word of the LORD was rare.
They lived and stood on their feet.

d: One of the most frequent verbs, over 1,500 times. Means to walk, go, travel, or metaphorically to live/conduct oneself. Walking with God is Bible's primary metaphor for faithful life — Enoch walked with God (Gen 5:24).
u: Hithpael form describes intimate, ongoing relationship with God. To halak in God's ways is summary of covenant obedience. Halakhah (Jewish legal tradition) derives from this — 'the way one walks.'
w: Range spans physical motion to moral direction. 'Walk in light,' 'walk in truth,' 'walk in integrity' — all halak expressions. God Himself 'walks' in garden (Gen 3:8) and among His people (Lev 26:12).
v: Blessed is the man who walks not in wickedness.

d: Hebrew interjection of vivid presentation, over 1,000 times. Means 'behold!' or 'look!' — signals something important, surprising, dramatic. Biblical narrator's way of making audience see, creating immediacy.
u: Prophets use to announce divine action: 'Behold, I am doing a new thing' (Isa 43:19). God reveals: 'Behold, a virgin shall conceive' (Isa 7:14). Creates theological suspense — when hinneh appears, pay attention.
w: First-person form hineni ('Here I am!') is faithful response to God's call — Abraham, Moses, Isaiah, Samuel all answer with hineni. Expresses total availability, surrender. Shift from 'Behold!' to 'Here I am!' captures revelation meeting response.
v: Behold, I am doing a new thing.
Behold, a ladder set up on earth.

d: Hebrew verb meaning to sing praises or make music, about 45 times. Denotes musical praise — singing with instruments. Related mizmor (psalm) appears in headings of 57 Psalms.
u: While halal is exuberant and yadah confessional, zamar is distinctly musical. Implies skilled, intentional worship. Music in Israel was not entertainment but theology set to melody.
w: Root may mean 'to pluck' (strings). Mizmor is psalm for musical performance. Levitical musicians were professionals of zamar (1 Chr 25). Eschatologically, all nations will zamar to God.
v: Sing praises to the LORD in Zion!
Sing praises to the LORD, O you his saints.

d: Hebrew verb meaning to be strong, strengthen, seize, about 290 times. Behind God's command to Joshua: 'Be strong and courageous.' Expresses divine empowerment for extraordinary faithfulness.
u: Appears at critical transitions. Moses charges Joshua (Deut 31:7). God tells Joshua (Josh 1:6-9). David tells Solomon (1 Chr 28:20). At every moment of new responsibility and fear, God's people commanded to draw strength from promises.
w: In Hiphil, means to harden — God 'hardened' Pharaoh's heart (Ex 4:21). In Hithpael, means to encourage oneself. Related chozek (strength) forms word family of divinely-sourced resilience.
v: Be strong and courageous. Do not fear.

d: Most common word for sin, about 300 times. From chata ('to miss the mark'). Remarkably, same word denotes both sin itself and sin offering atoning for it — problem and solution share one word.
u: Dual meaning is theologically profound. In Lev 4-5, chattath sacrifice addresses unintentional sins. Paul: God 'made him to be sin for us' (2 Cor 5:21) — Christ became sin offering.
w: Root ch-t-a literally 'to miss' (Judg 20:16 for slingers missing target). Sin conceived as missing God's mark. Eschatological promise: fountain opened for chattath (Zech 13:1).
v: He shall offer a bull as sin offering.
When his soul makes offering for guilt.
Blessed is the one whose sin is covered.

d: Hebrew word for living or life, about 500 times. God is 'living God' (El Chay), distinguishing from dead idols. 'Tree of life' bookends Scripture — Eden to New Jerusalem.
u: Life in OT is God's gift and domain. Only He gives chayyim. To choose God's covenant is to choose chayyim (Deut 30:19-20). Wisdom literature: fear of the LORD is 'fountain of chayyim' (Prov 14:27).
w: Oath 'as the LORD lives' (chay YHWH) invokes God's living reality. Ezekiel's dry bones (ch 37) is prophetic climax — God's power to resurrect dead nation. Eve's name (Chavvah) related — 'mother of all chay.'
v: I have set before you life and death. Choose life.
My soul thirsts for the living God.
The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life.
I will cause breath to enter you, and you shall live.
By this you know the living God is among you.

d: Wide semantic range, about 245 times. Means strength, capability, valor, wealth, military force. Famous eshet chayil ('woman of valor,' Prov 31:10) describes outstanding capability/character.
u: God's deliverance described in chayil language. Ruth called eshet chayil (3:11), Boaz ish gibbor chayil (2:1). Zechariah: 'Not by might [chayil], but by my Spirit' (4:6) — redefines strength.
w: Root may relate to chay (life), suggesting vital energy. Contexts: military force (Ex 14:4), personal wealth (Deut 8:18), physical strength (1 Sam 2:4), moral excellence (Prov 31:10).
v: An excellent woman [eshet chayil] who can find?
Not by might [chayil], but by my Spirit.
He gives you power to get wealth.
The king is not saved by his great army.

d: Hebrew word for wisdom, about 150 times. Far more than intellectual knowledge — practical skill for living well, moral discernment, ultimately the fear of the LORD. In Prov 8, Wisdom personified, present at creation.
u: Solomon asked for chokmah (1 Kgs 3:9). Wisdom tradition explores from multiple angles. Personification in Prov 8 — present before creation, delighting in mankind — anticipates Logos theology of John 1.
w: Root produces chakam (wise person), chokmah (wisdom), chokmoth (teachings). Applies to artisans (skill in craftsmanship), rulers (wisdom to govern), ordinary people (skill in living). True chokmah begins and ends with fear of the LORD.
v: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.
The LORD possessed me [Wisdom] at the beginning.
I give you a wise and discerning mind.
Behold, the fear of the Lord, that is wisdom.

d: Derived from chesed (lovingkindness), describes one practicing covenant faithfulness — godly/devout person. About 32 times. Plural chasidim became name for Hasidic movement.
u: The chasid is ideal covenant partner — faithful to God, loyal to community. Psalms appeal for His chasidim: 'He preserves the lives of his saints' (Ps 97:10). Because God is chasid (Ps 145:17), His people become chasidim.
w: Relationship between chesed (virtue) and chasid (person embodying it) is instructive. Chesed is God's covenantal love; chasid receives and mirrors it. Concept influenced Second Temple Judaism, giving rise to Hasidim resisting Hellenization.
v: Preserve my life, for I am godly.
He preserves the lives of his saints.
Praise in the assembly of the godly.
The LORD is faithful in all he does.
With the faithful you show yourself faithful.

d: Hebrew word for statute or decree, about 130 times. Denotes prescribed limit, boundary, regulation — whether divine law, royal decree, or natural ordinance. Emphasizes fixed, established nature of God's commands.
u: In Torah vocabulary, one of several words for divine commands (alongside mitsvah, mishpat, torah). Psalm 119 uses all these synonyms. Conveys laws inscribed, permanent, non-negotiable. God also sets choq for creation — boundaries for sea, patterns for rain.
w: Root ch-q-q means 'to engrave, inscribe, decree.' A choq is literally something cut into stone — permanent and authoritative. Unlike mishpat (judicial decisions) or mitsvah (commanded duty), emphasizes inscribed permanence.
v: Oh that my ways were steadfast in keeping your statutes!
Who gives the fixed order [chuqqoth] of the moon.
The LORD commanded us to do all these statutes.

d: Fundamental word for good, over 740 times. The word God uses to evaluate creation — 'God saw that it was good' (Gen 1). Encompasses moral goodness, aesthetic beauty, practical usefulness, experiential pleasure.
u: Breadth reflects Hebrew conviction that goodness is holistic. Psalms celebrate: 'Taste and see that the LORD is tov' (34:8). 'Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me' (23:6). Absence of tov is essence of Fall.
w: 'It is not tov for man to be alone' (Gen 2:18) is God's first negative assessment — loneliness first thing declared 'not good.' Establishes tov includes relational wholeness. Root produces tuvah (goodness), name Tobiah ('God is good').
v: God saw everything, and behold, it was very good.
Oh, taste and see that the LORD is good!
Surely goodness and mercy shall follow me.
Behold, how good and pleasant when brothers dwell in unity!
The LORD is good, a stronghold in trouble.

d: Hebrew verb meaning to give thanks, praise, confess, about 115 times. Related to yad (hand), suggesting praise with outstretched hands. Name Judah derives from yadah — 'This time I will praise' (Gen 29:35).
u: Hiphil form means 'to give thanks, confess.' Responds to God's mighty acts. Refrain 'Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good' (Ps 136) uses yadah. In confession, means to acknowledge truth — including sin (Prov 28:13). Dual meaning reflects that true worship requires honesty.
w: Name Judah (Yehudah) contains divine name within yadah — praising God written into leading tribe's name. In Hithpael, means confess — Daniel's prayer (9:4) and Ezra's (10:1) use yadah for national sin.
v: Give thanks to the LORD, for he is good.
'This time I will praise the LORD.' So she named him Judah.
I will give thanks to the LORD with my whole heart.

d: Hebrew word for fear or reverence, about 45 times. From yare, 'to fear.' Encompasses terror to reverent awe. 'Fear of the LORD' (yirath YHWH) is keystone of wisdom — 'The beginning of wisdom is the fear of the LORD' (Prov 9:10).
u: Biblical yirah is not fright but proper orientation to the holy — awe, reverence, wonder before God's majesty. This fear liberates: one who fears God need not fear anything else (Ps 112:1, 7). Yirath YHWH is 'clean, enduring forever' (Ps 19:9).
w: Verb yare appears over 330 times. God's 'Do not fear' (al-tira) appears alongside demand to fear Him — paradox: fear God and fear nothing else. 'God-fearer' (yere Elohim) became term for devout Gentiles.
v: The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge.
The fear of the LORD is clean, enduring forever.
The fear of the LORD is a fountain of life.
The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.

d: Hebrew adjective meaning upright or straight, about 120 times. Describes moral integrity. Poetic name Yeshurun ('the upright one') derives from this root.
u: A yashar person walks honestly, deals fairly. 'The upright shall behold his face' (Ps 11:7). Judges ends: 'Everyone did what was right in his own eyes' (21:25) — absence of divine standard.
w: Root y-sh-r means 'to be level, straight.' Produces mesharim (equity), mishor (level ground). In Proverbs, yashar path contrasts with crooked way.
v: Every way is right in his own eyes.
Everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
A way that seems right but ends in death.

d: Hebrew verb for salvation, about 205 times. Root behind Joshua, Isaiah, and Jesus (Greek form of Yeshua). Encompasses rescue from physical danger, oppression, and ultimately sin.
u: God is primary subject: 'The LORD saved Israel that day' (Ex 14:30). The judges are moshiim (deliverers). Prophetic promise: 'Your king comes, having salvation' (Zech 9:9). Bridges temporal deliverance and eternal salvation.
w: Word family: yeshuah (salvation), teshuah (deliverance), moshia (savior). Yeshua (Jesus) means 'he will save' — connecting entire OT vocabulary of deliverance to Christ.
v: Turn to me and be saved, all the ends of the earth!
The LORD is my rock and deliverer.
The LORD raised up judges who saved them.

d: Hebrew word for strength or power, about 130 times. Describes physical strength, capacity, or resources. Applies to human ability, natural forces, and supremely to God's limitless power.
u: God gives koach to the weary (Isa 40:29). He gives Israel koach to get wealth (Deut 8:18). Contrast between human and divine koach is recurring theme: 'Not by might, nor by power' (Zech 4:6).
w: Appears in contexts of physical exertion (Judg 16:5, Samson's strength), productive capacity (Gen 4:12), divine omnipotence (Job 26:14). Renewal of koach through waiting on the LORD (Isa 40:31) is beloved promise.
v: They who wait for the LORD shall renew their strength.
In your hand are power and might.
Great is our Lord, abundant in power.
He gives you power to get wealth.

d: Hebrew word for commandment, about 180 times. From verb tsavah (to command). Denotes specific divine command — obligation placed on God's people as part of covenant. Jewish tradition counts 613 mitsvoth in Torah.
u: A mitsvah is not arbitrary law but covenantal instruction — God's way of ordering life toward flourishing. 'This commandment is not too hard for you' (Deut 30:11). Psalmist loves God's mitsvoth: 'I will delight in your commandments' (Ps 119:47).
w: In rabbinic Judaism, evolved to mean any good deed. But biblically refers specifically to God's commands. Plural mitsvoth appears alongside chuqqim (statutes), mishpatim (judgments), torah (instruction) as part of God's revealed will.
v: It will be righteousness if we observe all these commandments.
This commandment is not too hard for you.

d: One of the most frequent verbs, about 2,015 times. Means to give, grant, put, place, allow. God is supreme Giver — He gives the land (Gen 12:7), the Torah (Ex 24:12), wisdom (1 Kgs 3:12), and ultimately His Son. Name Nathan = 'God has given.'
u: Everything Israel has is nathan — given by God. Land, law, priesthood, kingship — all divine gifts. Theology of gift prevents pride: 'What do you have that you did not receive?' Prophetic promise extends giving: God will nathan a new heart and spirit (Ezek 36:26).
w: Remarkably flexible: 'give' a hand (help), 'give' the heart (pay attention), 'give' glory (worship), 'give' a cry (shout). When God 'gives' His people into enemies' hands, it means delivering to judgment. When He 'gives' them land, it means fulfilling promise.
v: To your offspring I will give this land.
I will give you a new heart.
He has put eternity into man's heart.

d: One of the most common verbs, about 2,630 times. Means to do, make, produce, or accomplish. While bara is reserved for divine creation and yatsar for shaping, asah is general verb of making — used for both God and humans.
u: God asah the heavens and earth (Gen 2:4), the luminaries (1:16), all living things. Humans asah garments, tools, sacrifices, covenants. Ethical imperative is to asah justice and righteousness (Gen 18:19, Mic 6:8). 'To hear and to do' — shama and asah — summarizes faithful covenant response.
w: Carries sense of practical accomplishment. God's word does not return void but accomplishes (asah) its purpose (Isa 55:11). Phrase asah chesed ('do lovingkindness') shows chesed is not passive sentiment but active deed. 'Doers of the word' is concept rooted in this verb.
v: To do justice, love kindness, walk humbly.
This is the day the LORD has made.
It shall accomplish that which I purpose.
They shall keep the way of the LORD by doing righteousness.

d: Hebrew word for face or presence, over 2,100 times. Panim (always plural) refers to face of a person, front of something, or presence of God. Panim of God is His personal, relational presence — ultimate blessing or terror depending on standing.
u: Aaronic blessing: 'The LORD make his face shine upon you' (Num 6:25). Seeking God's face means pursuing His presence: 'Seek my face' (Ps 27:8). God's face turned away means favor withdrawn. 'Bread of the Presence' literally 'bread of the face' — always before God. Moses spoke 'face to face' (Ex 33:11) — pinnacle of intimacy.
w: Plural form suggests multiple expressions of a face. To 'set the face toward' means to resolve firmly. To 'fall on the face' means to prostrate. To 'lift someone's face' means to show favoritism. Rich idiom makes it one of Hebrew's most expressive words.
v: The LORD make his face shine upon you.
'Seek my face.' My heart says, 'Your face, LORD, do I seek.'
The LORD spoke to Moses face to face.
If my people seek my face, I will heal their land.

d: Hebrew word for transgression or rebellion, about 95 times. Strongest of three main sin words. While chattath is missing the mark and avon is crookedness, pesha is deliberate rebellion — willful breaking of relationship with God.
u: A pesha is not accident but revolt. When Israel 'transgressed' the covenant (Josh 7:11), they consciously chose disloyalty. Prophets charge Israel with pesha as covenant rebellion: 'For three transgressions and for four...' (Amos 1-2). Yet even pesha is forgivable — God 'pardons transgression' (Mic 7:18).
w: Implies broken relationship requiring reconciliation. In political usage, describes vassal's rebellion against suzerain (2 Kgs 3:7). Applied to divine-human relationship, depicts sin at its most defiant. Suffering Servant was 'wounded for our transgressions' (Isa 53:5).
v: Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven.
Who is a God like you, pardoning transgression?

d: Hebrew word for righteousness, about 120 times. Tsedek (and related feminine tsedaqah) denotes conformity to standard — living rightly in relationship with God and others. Combines what English separates into 'righteousness' (moral quality) and 'justice' (social action).
u: God's tsedek is His righteous character — the standard by which all else is measured. Messiah will reign in tsedek (Isa 32:1). Tsedek and mishpat (justice) form word pair: 'Righteousness and justice are the foundation of your throne' (Ps 89:14). Society ordered by tsedek protects vulnerable, reflects God's character.
w: Name Melchizedek means 'king of tsedek.' Word applies to accurate weights (Deut 25:15 — 'righteous weights'), legal verdicts, ethical conduct. Prophetic demand for tsedek (Amos 5:24 — 'let justice roll down like waters') is simultaneously social and spiritual.
v: Righteousness and justice are foundation of your throne.
Let justice roll down like waters, righteousness like a stream.
He believed, and he counted it as righteousness.

d: One of the most common verbs, about 740 times. Means to call, call out, proclaim, summon, or read aloud. In OT, naming is act of authority, calling on God's name is act of faith. God qara the light 'Day' (Gen 1:5) — naming expresses sovereignty over creation.
u: To qara on the name of the LORD (Gen 4:26) is to worship — acknowledging God publicly. God qara Israel by name (Isa 43:1) — summoning into relationship. Prophets qara (proclaim) God's word. Public reading (qara) of Torah (Neh 8) is foundational act of covenant renewal.
w: Bridges several speech acts: naming, summoning, invoking, proclaiming, reading. When God calls, it creates reality — 'I have called you by name; you are mine' (Isa 43:1). When humans call on God, it expresses dependence — 'Call upon me in the day of trouble' (Ps 50:15). Communication is at heart of covenant relationship.
v: I have called you by name, you are mine.
Call upon me in the day of trouble.
Everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved.
Call to me and I will answer you.

d: One of the most theologically rich words, about 380 times. Ruach means wind, breath, or spirit — and genius of Hebrew is that all three meanings overlap. Spirit of God (ruach Elohim) hovered over waters at creation (Gen 1:2). Human ruach is God-given life-force.
u: God's ruach is His active presence — creating (Gen 1:2), empowering leaders (Judg 3:10), inspiring prophets (Num 11:25), renewing hearts (Ezek 36:27). Prophetic hope is outpouring of ruach on all flesh (Joel 2:28). Ruach cannot be controlled — 'The wind blows where it wishes' (Eccl 11:5).
w: Threefold meaning creates rich wordplay. In Ezek 37, God commands ruach (wind/breath/spirit) to breathe on dry bones — all three senses activate simultaneously. In Ps 51:10-12, David prays for new ruach (spirit of willingness), God's holy ruach (Spirit), willing ruach (attitude). Resists reduction to single meaning.
v: The Spirit of God was hovering over the waters.
I will put my Spirit within you.
I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh.
Do not take your Holy Spirit from me.
Come from the four winds, O breath.

d: Hebrew word for heavens or sky, appearing over 420 times. Always plural in form (shamayim). The opening verse pairs shamayim with erets (earth) — 'In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth.' Encompasses atmospheric sky, celestial realm, and God's throne.
u: 'The heavens declare the glory of God' (Ps 19:1) — the visible shamayim testify to invisible majesty. 'Heaven is my throne' (Isa 66:1) — the transcendent shamayim is God's dwelling. Prophetic vision extends: 'new heavens and new earth' (Isa 65:17) — eschatological renewal encompasses all creation.
w: The plural form may suggest fullness or may reflect ancient cosmology of multiple tiers. Phrases like 'heaven of heavens' (Deut 10:14) use intensive plural. While erets is humanity's domain, shamayim is God's — yet the two intersect in worship, prayer, and divine revelation.
v: In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth.
The heavens declare the glory of God.
Heaven is my throne, and the earth is my footstool.
Behold, to the LORD belong heaven and the heaven of heavens.
Behold, I create new heavens and a new earth.

d: One of the most important Hebrew verbs, appearing about 1,165 times. Shama means to hear, listen, or obey — and remarkably, all three meanings reside in one word. True hearing leads to obedience; mere auditory reception without response is not biblical shama.
u: The Shema ('Hear, O Israel,' Deut 6:4) is the central confession of Jewish faith, named for this verb. To shama the Torah means not just to hear it read but to obey its commands. God's appeal through the prophets is always for Israel to shama — 'If you will listen diligently to the voice of the LORD your God' (Deut 28:1). Prayer begins with the conviction that God shama — 'You who hear prayer' (Ps 65:2).
w: The integration of hearing and obeying in one word is theologically profound. Ancient Hebrew culture was oral-aural; the primary mode of receiving Torah was hearing it proclaimed. To truly hear meant to internalize and act. Jesus' parable of the sower (the seed is the word) assumes this Hebrew understanding: some hear but do not truly shama; others hear and bear fruit.
v: Hear, O Israel: The LORD our God, the LORD is one.
Incline your ear, and come to me; hear, that your soul may live.
O you who hear prayer, to you shall all flesh come.
If you diligently listen to the voice of the LORD.

d: One of the most significant Hebrew words, appearing about 220 times. Torah means instruction, teaching, or law. Far richer than English 'law' — it is God's gracious instruction for life. From the verb yarah (to instruct, point the way), torah is divine guidance, not arbitrary legislation.
u: Torah is God's gift, not burden. Psalm 1 celebrates the one who delights in the torah of the LORD. Psalm 119 — the longest chapter in the Bible — is an extended meditation on the beauty, wisdom, and life-giving power of torah. 'Your torah is my delight' (Ps 119:77). The Law is not opposed to grace; it is grace — God revealing how to live in covenant with Him.
w: Torah can refer to: (1) the first five books of Moses (the Pentateuch); (2) all divine instruction in Scripture; (3) a specific law or commandment; (4) priestly instruction. The concept is not 'legalism' but 'discipleship.' Jesus did not abolish the torah but fulfilled it (Matt 5:17) — bringing its deepest meaning to light.
v: His delight is in the law of the LORD.
Oh how I love your law! It is my meditation all the day.
The commandment is a lamp and the teaching a light.
This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth.
They read from the Book of the Law of God, clearly.

d: A Hebrew adjective meaning complete, whole, blameless, or without blemish, appearing about 90 times. Tamim describes both moral integrity and physical perfection. Sacrificial animals must be tamim — without defect (Lev 1:3). Covenant partners are called to be tamim — walking blamelessly before God (Gen 17:1).
u: God commands Abraham: 'Walk before me, and be blameless' (Gen 17:1). Noah was tamim in his generation (Gen 6:9) — not sinless, but marked by integrity. The tamim person is wholehearted, undivided, living in covenant faithfulness. Job is described as tamim (Job 1:1) — his integrity is the foundation of the book's drama.
w: The root t-m-m conveys completeness and wholeness. Tamim is not perfection in the sense of flawlessness but wholeness in the sense of integrity — one's life matches one's profession. The word applies to God's way (Ps 18:30), God's work (Deut 32:4), and to those who walk in His covenant. The NT concept of teleios (mature, complete) in Matt 5:48 echoes tamim.
v: You shall be blameless before the LORD your God.
This God—his way is perfect; the word of the LORD proves true.
He shall offer a male without blemish.

d: The Hebrew word for hope or expectation, appearing about 34 times. Tikvah is not wishful thinking but confident expectation rooted in God's character and promises. The word also means 'cord' or 'line' (from the root qavah, to wait, bind together) — hope is the cord that binds the present to the future.
u: Hope in the OT is never abstract. It is hope in the LORD (Ps 25:5, 39:7, 71:5). 'Why are you cast down, O my soul? Hope in God' (Ps 42:5). The tikvah of Israel is bound to God's covenant faithfulness. In exile, hope sustains: 'There is hope for your future' (Jer 31:17). The righteous have tikvah even in death (Prov 14:32).
w: Rahab's scarlet cord (tikvah, Josh 2:18, 21) becomes a symbol of hope — the means of deliverance for her household. The wordplay is intentional: the 'cord' of deliverance is also the 'hope' of salvation. Tikvah is active waiting, not passive resignation. It is confident expectation that God will act.
v: Plans to give you a future and a hope.
And now, O Lord, for what do I wait? My hope is in you.
Surely there is a future, and your hope will not be cut off.
But this I call to mind, and therefore I have hope.

Even if I should have a husband tonight and bear sons, would you therefore wait until they were grown? No, my daughters, for it is exceedingly bitter to me for your sake that the hand of the LORD has gone out against me.
